title: STRONG Support for Children Act of 2025
short_title: STRONG Support for Children Act of 2025
plain_summary: The STRONG Support for Children Act of 2025 creates two programs that give money to help kids who have been through scary or harmful experiences. The first program gives money to state and local health departments so they can use computer information to find places where many children have had bad experiences and help connect their families to services that can help them. The second program gives money to local governments and tribes so they can work together to provide care services for young children and their families who might experience trauma. Both programs are designed to prevent trauma from happening to children and to help families get the support they need when trauma does happen.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Services and Trauma-informed Research of Outcomes in Neighborhoods Grants for Support for Children Act of 2025 or the STRONG Support for Children Act of 2025.

sections.title: Section 2 Data Analysis and Strategy Implementation to Prevent and Mitigate Childhood Trauma
sections.text:
This section adds a new program to the Public Health Service Act. The Secretary of Health and Human Services must create a program that does two main things. First, it supports the creation and use of programs that analyze data to find and help implement strategies for early help and prevention. These strategies aim to prevent and reduce childhood trauma while supporting communities and families. This includes improving connections through care coordination, aligning community efforts in areas that need help most, and expanding community capacity through collaboration across different sectors. Second, the program evaluates how well these programs work to improve outcomes for children.

The Secretary will award grants to up to five eligible organizations to carry out these activities. Grant money must be used to support programs that use data analysis methods to identify and help implement strategies for early intervention and prevention to prevent and reduce childhood trauma and support communities and families.

Grant recipients must use data analysis methods to identify specific geographic areas like census tracts that have high rates of adverse childhood experiences and significant risk factors for poor outcomes for children. These areas might have high rates of poor public health outcomes including illness, disease, suicide, and death. They might also have high rates of exclusionary discipline practices like suspensions, expulsions, and referrals to law enforcement, as well as low graduation rates. Other indicators include high rates of substance use disorders, poverty, foster system involvement or referrals, housing instability and homelessness, food insecurity, inequity including disparities in income, wealth, employment, educational attainment, health care access, and public health outcomes based on race, sex, sexuality and gender identity, ethnicity, or nationality, incarceration rates, or other indicators of adversity as defined by the Secretary.

Grant recipients must also identify strategies to improve outcomes for children aged zero through seventeen that build on strengths in communities that could be further supported. This includes existing support networks for families and enhanced connections to community-based organizations.

Grant recipients must implement strategies to help reach out to and involve children and their caregivers in federal, state, or local programs that provide reparative, gender-responsive, culturally specific, and trauma-informed prevention services for which children and their caregivers are eligible. These programs include home visiting programs, training and education on parenting skills, substance use disorder prevention and treatment that is voluntary and noncoercive, mental health supports and care that is voluntary and noncoercive, family and intimate partner violence prevention services, child advocacy center programming, economic and nutrition support services, housing support services including emergency and temporary shelter for those experiencing homelessness and housing insecurity as well as stable long-term housing, voluntary noncoercive gender-responsive and culturally specific mental health supports in school and early childhood education center-based settings, wraparound programs for transitioning youth and youth currently in the foster system, programming to support the health and well-being of lesbian gay bisexual transgender and intersex children and their families, and family resource center services.

The Secretary cannot award a grant for a service if that service is already available under the state Medicaid plan unless the state and all eligible subdivisions involved will enter into agreements with public or nonprofit private entities to provide the service and demonstrate that these entities will seek payment according to the usual payment schedule under the state plan and have entered into a participation agreement.

Grant recipients can use up to twenty-five percent of grant money during the first twenty-four months to use data analysis methods to identify specific geographic areas where services will be provided and to identify support and intervention services to improve outcomes for children in those areas. In subsequent years, they can use up to ten percent of the grant to continue data analysis activities.

Grant recipients cannot use more than five percent of grant money for administration, reporting, and program oversight functions including developing systems to improve data collection and data sharing.

When awarding grants, the Secretary must give priority to eligible entities that use community-based system dynamic modeling as the primary data analysis method. System dynamic modeling means a method of data analysis and predictive modeling that includes using community-based participatory research methods for involving community in understanding and changing systems and evaluating grant outcomes, considering multiple environmental risk factors and determining the significance of contributing community risk factors to identify strategies to reduce adverse child outcomes including maltreatment cases, involvement with the juvenile criminal legal system or foster system, exclusionary school discipline, or exposure to violence, and identifying cross-sector responses involving reparative trauma-informed culturally specific gender-responsive and community-based organizations to reduce adverse child outcomes.

Grant recipients must use at least twenty-five percent of the total grant amount to make subgrants to organizations that help implement the strategy for preventing and mitigating childhood trauma and supporting communities and families. To be eligible for a subgrant, an organization must submit an application showing that the needs of the population to be served are urgent and are not met by currently available services in the geographic area and that the organization can provide the required services. Subgrant funds must be used to supplement and not replace state or local funds.

To get a grant, eligible entities must submit an application demonstrating that the applicant uses trauma-informed culturally specific and gender-responsive practices including showing the extent to which the applicant has trained staff in these practices, that the applicant can administer the grant including conducting all required data analysis activities, and that services will be provided to children and families in an accessible culturally relevant and linguistically specific manner consistent with local needs. The application must include a preliminary analysis of how the applicant will use the grant to identify geographic areas to be served using data analysis methods, use data analysis methods to identify strategies to improve outcomes for children in the geographic area, facilitate strategies through care coordination efforts, and track data for evaluation of outcomes. The application must also include a detailed project plan for grant use including anticipated technical assistance needs and additional funding sources including state and local funds supporting the prevention and mitigation of adverse childhood experiences.

Grants cannot exceed nine million five hundred thousand dollars and cannot last longer than seven years.

As a condition of receiving a grant, eligible entities must agree that any assistance provided to an individual through the grant will be provided without regard to the individual's ability to pay for services, current or past health condition, immigration status, sexual orientation and gender identity, or any prior involvement in the criminal legal system.

Grant recipients cannot use grant money to use data analysis methods to inform individual case decisions including child removal or placement decisions or to target services at certain individuals or families, require any individual or family to participate in any service or program as a condition of receiving a benefit to which they are otherwise eligible, increase the presence or funding of law enforcement surveillance involvement or activity in implementing the identified strategies, or enable the practice of conversion therapy.

The Assistant Secretary for Planning and Evaluation of the Department of Health and Human Services must complete evaluations of the program. Within thirty-six months after enactment, there must be an evaluation of the effectiveness of the data model accuracy of the grant program. Within six years after enactment, there must be an evaluation of the effectiveness of the entire grant program. Within seven years after enactment, the Assistant Secretary must complete a study on the results of the grant program using community-based participatory action research method and submit a report to Congress.

Within three months after completing the program evaluation, the Assistant Secretary must submit to Congress and make available to the public a report that includes the impact of the program on homelessness and housing insecurity, substance use disorder and drug deaths, incarceration, foster system involvement, and other child and family outcomes, an analysis of which elements of the program should be replicated and scaled by governmental or non-governmental entities, and recommendations for legislation and administrative action.

The section includes detailed definitions of adverse childhood experience, eligible entity, and the practice of conversion therapy. An adverse childhood experience means a potentially traumatic experience that occurs in childhood and can have a tremendous impact on the child's lifelong health and opportunity outcomes. This includes various forms of abuse, household challenges like domestic violence or substance use disorders, neglect, living in impoverished communities, experiencing food insecurity, witnessing violence, foster system involvement, experiencing discrimination, dealing with historical and ongoing traumas due to systemic racism or other forms of discrimination, dealing with deportation threats, impacts of multigenerational poverty, and living through natural disasters. An eligible entity means a state or local health department. The practice of conversion therapy means any practice or treatment that seeks to change another individual's sexual orientation or gender identity for monetary compensation, but does not include practices that provide assistance with gender transition or acceptance and support.

For fiscal years 2025 through 2032, forty-seven million five hundred thousand dollars is authorized to be appropriated for grants plus necessary funds for administrative costs, and seven million five hundred thousand dollars is authorized for evaluations.

sections.title: Section 3 Care Coordination Grants
sections.text:
This section adds a new program to the Public Health Service Act for care coordination grants. The Secretary of Health and Human Services will award grants to eligible entities to establish or expand trauma-informed care coordination services to support children aged zero through five who are at risk of adverse childhood experiences and their caregivers including prenatal people of any age.

The Secretary must award at least nine and no more than forty grants under this section, subject to available funding. Each grant must be at least two hundred fifty thousand dollars and no more than one million dollars per fiscal year.

To be eligible for a grant, an entity must be a local government or Indian Tribe acting through their public health department if they have one.

When awarding grants, the Secretary must give priority to eligible entities proposing to serve communities with high need for trauma-informed care coordination services. This need is demonstrated by indicators such as pregnant people who face barriers to prenatal care, high death rates of people giving birth or infants, caretakers and parents living with mental health conditions or substance use disorders, high rates of community violence including domestic violence, high proportions of low-income children, high rates of child fatalities or near fatalities related to child abuse and neglect, significant disparities in health outcomes for people giving birth and infants, high rates of exclusionary discipline and referrals to law enforcement, and high rates of homelessness and housing instability. The Secretary working with the Director of the Indian Health Service must consult with Indian Tribes to establish criteria to measure these indicators for Tribal areas.

Grants must be used to establish or expand gender-responsive culturally specific trauma-informed care coordination services including conducting risk and needs assessments using strengths-based approaches focused on protective factors for children and their caregivers including prenatal people of any age, and inputting screening results into a centralized intake system to promote a single point of access system across providers and services. Grants must also be used to ensure that individuals employed through grant funds receive sufficient and up-to-date training on trauma-informed care and strategies that are reparative culturally sensitive gender-responsive and healing-centered.

Grants may also be used for employing care coordinators case managers community health workers certified infant mental health specialists and outreach and engagement specialists to work with children and their caregivers including prenatal individuals to prevent and respond to adverse childhood experiences by connecting clients with culturally specific trauma-informed care treatment services including economic social food and housing supports. Grant money can also be used for providing training to community health providers and community partners, expanding enhancing modifying and connecting existing networks of community programs and services to achieve a more comprehensive and coordinated system of care approach, compiling information on available resources and referral services through community-based organizations and local state and federal agencies, establishing or updating databases to track the effectiveness of care coordination services, developing and implementing referral partnership agreements with various organizations and providers, supporting cross-system planning and collaboration among employees in different sectors, providing or subsidizing services to address barriers that children prenatal individuals and caregivers face in utilizing community resources and services, and creating or expanding infrastructure and investing in technology including providing communications technology and internet service to enable increased telemedicine capabilities.

For Indian tribes, the Secretary may waive provisions as determined appropriate.

Grant recipients cannot use grant money to use data analysis methods to inform individual case decisions including child removal or placement decisions or to target services at certain individuals or families, require any individual or family to participate in any service or program as a condition of receiving a benefit to which they are otherwise eligible, or increase the presence or funding of law enforcement surveillance involvement or activity in connection with trauma-informed care coordination services.

As conditions of receiving grants, eligible entities must agree that they will not use more than thirty percent of grant funds to establish or update a database, ensure that all care coordination services are provided in accessible settings including mobile settings for low-income or no-income individuals and individuals in rural areas with at least fifty percent of services occurring in community settings convenient to those being served, use grants to supplement not replace other federal state or local funds, maintain confidentiality of individuals receiving services, partner with community-based organizations with experience serving child populations prenatally through age five, coordinate with the local agency responsible for administering the state Medicaid plan, and employ risk stratification to develop different effective models of care for different populations based on their needs.

To apply for grants, eligible entities must submit applications containing goals to be achieved, number of individuals likely to be served including demographic data, existing programs and services that can be used to increase the proportion of children and families who receive needed supports and services, a plan for expanding coordinating or modifying existing networks of programs and services, demonstration of ability to reach individuals to be served including by partnering with local stakeholders, indication of how personnel are reflective of communities to be served, and a list of stakeholders with whom the entity plans to partner or consult.

Within four years after enactment, grant recipients must submit reports to the Secretary describing activities funded through grants including number of individuals served, number of referrals made and their success rates, qualitative analysis of collaborative partnerships, number of services provided, aggregated and de-identified outcomes experienced by individuals served, and other information required by the Secretary.

After all grants conclude, the Assistant Secretary for Planning and Evaluation must provide opportunities for program participants to share lessons learned challenges experienced and ideas for next steps and solutions. The Secretary must then compile findings and conclusions of grantees, submit a report to appropriate congressional committees, and make the report publicly available.

The section includes definitions of adverse childhood experience, care coordination, Indian Tribe, and protective factors. Care coordination means an active ongoing process that assists children ages zero through five at risk of or who have experienced adverse childhood experiences and their caregivers including prenatal people to identify access and use community resources and services, is client-centered and comprehensive, ensures closed loop referrals by obtaining feedback from families served, and works across systems and services to promote collaboration to effectively meet community needs. Protective factors refer to supportive elements in a child or caretaker's life that help them withstand trauma such as stable school environments or supportive peer relationships.

Fifteen million dollars is authorized to be appropriated for each of the five fiscal years following enactment. At least ten percent of available funds must be used for grants to Indian tribes. Up to fifteen percent may be used for administrative expenses and up to five percent may be reserved for technical assistance to help eligible entities prepare and submit applications.
